content:
# Philippians Chapter 4 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph), PDF pages 196-197 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_004_page_001.jpg, PHP_004_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** So-then, my brothers, beloved and longed-for, my joy and crown, thus stand-firm in [the] Lord, beloved-ones.

**2.** Euodia I-exhort and Syntyche I-exhort the same [thing] to-think in [the] Lord.

**3.** Yes, I-ask also you, genuine yokefellow, assist them, who in the good-news contended-together with me, with also Clement and the rest of my fellow-workers, whose names [are] in [the] book of life.

**4.** Rejoice in [the] Lord always; again I-will-say, rejoice.

**5.** Let your gentleness be-known to all men. The Lord [is] near.

**6.** Be-anxious-about nothing, but in everything by the prayer and the supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be-made-known before God.

**7.** And the peace of God, the [one] surpassing every mind, shall-guard your hearts and your thoughts in Christ Jesus.

**8.** For-the-rest, brothers, whatever-things are true, whatever-things [are] honorable, whatever-things [are] righteous, whatever-things [are] pure, whatever-things [are] lovely, whatever-things [are] well-spoken-of, if [there is] any virtue and if any praise, these-things reckon;

**9.** which-things also you-learned and received and heard and saw in me, these-things practice; and the God of peace shall-be with you.

**10.** But I-rejoiced in [the] Lord greatly, that now at-last you-revived the thinking on-behalf-of me, upon which also you-were-thinking, but you-were-lacking-opportunity.

**11.** Not that according-to lack I-speak, for I have-learned in whatever [circumstances] I-am, self-sufficient to-be.

**12.** I-know also to-be-humbled, I-know also to-abound; in everything and in all-things I-have-been-initiated, both to-be-filled and to-hunger, both to-abound and to-lack.

**13.** I-am-strong-for all-things in the [one] empowering me.

**14.** Nevertheless, well you-did, having-shared-together with my affliction.

**15.** And you-know also, you Philippians, that in [the] beginning of the good-news, when I-went-out from Macedonia, no assembly shared-with me in [the] account of giving and receiving except you alone;

**16.** because also in Thessalonica both once and twice you-sent to my need.

**17.** Not that I-seek-after the gift, but I-seek-after the fruit, the [one] abounding to your account.

**18.** But I-have-received all-things and I-abound; I-have-been-filled, having-received from Epaphroditus the [things] from you, a fragrance of sweet-smell, a sacrifice acceptable, well-pleasing to God.

**19.** And my God shall-fill-full your every need according-to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.

**20.** And to our God and Father [be] the glory to the ages of the ages; amen.

**21.** Greet every holy-one in Christ Jesus. The brothers, the [ones] with me, greet you.

**22.** All the holy-ones greet you, and especially the [ones] of the household of Caesar.

**23.** The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ [be] with your spirit.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **4:3 -- "genuine yokefellow" (gnhsie suzuge):** Sinaiticus reads ΓΝΗΣΙΕ ΣΥΖΥΓΕ. Some scholars have debated whether "Syzygus" is a proper name, but the adjective ΓΝΗΣΙΕ ("genuine") strongly suggests it is a common noun meaning "yokefellow" or "companion." The Sinaiticus text is clear here.

2. **4:7 -- "mind" (noun):** Sinaiticus reads ΝΟΥΝ (mind/understanding) rather than the later Byzantine reading ΝΟΗΜΑ. The word ΝΟΥΝ denotes the faculty of rational thought, hence "every mind" -- the peace of God surpasses all human intellectual capacity.

3. **4:13 -- Omission of "Christ":** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΕΝΔΥΝΑΜΟΥΝΤΙ ΜΕ ("in the one empowering me") without the addition of ΧΡΙΣΤΩ ("Christ"), which appears in many later manuscripts. This is a significant textual variant. The shorter reading is generally considered more original by modern text critics. The Byzantine/Majority text tradition adds "Christ" to produce the familiar "I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me."

4. **4:16 -- "both once and twice":** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΙ ΑΠΑΞ ΚΑΙ ΔΙΣ, a Hebraic idiom meaning "more than once" or "repeatedly," rendered literally here.

5. **4:23 -- "your spirit":** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥ ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ ("your spirit," singular-collective). Some later manuscripts add ΑΜΗΝ at the close. Sinaiticus does not include the subscriptio found in many later manuscripts attributing the letter's origin to Rome.

6. **4:3 -- "contended-together" (sunhqlhsan):** The verb ΣΥΝΗΘΛΗΣΑΝ is an athletic metaphor -- they "competed alongside" Paul in the struggle of gospel proclamation. Rendered literally rather than smoothing to "worked with."

7. **4:12 -- "I-have-been-initiated" (memuhmai):** ΜΕΜΥΗΜΑΙ is a perfect passive from ΜΥΕΩ, a term from the Greek mystery religions meaning "to be initiated into secret rites." Paul repurposes this cultic vocabulary -- he has been "initiated" into the secret of contentment through all circumstances.

-- EMET
